**Ziqi Kang^1^, Angela Szabo^1^, Teodora Farago^1^, Fernando
Perez-Villatoro^1^, Inga-Maria Launonen^1^, Ella Anttila^1^, Kevin
Elias^2^, Julia Casado^1^, Peter K. Sorger^3^, Anniina
Färkkilä^1,\ 4,\ 5,\ 6^**

**Author Biographies:**

**Ziqi Kang** is a PhD student in bioinformatics and computational
biology at University of Helsinki, Finland. Her research is focusing on
profiling the tumor microenvironment.

**Angela Szabo** is a bioinformatician in multiplexed immunofluorescence
image analysis at University of Helsinki.

**Teodora Farago** is a bioinformatician in multiplexed
immunofluorescence image analysis at University of Helsinki.

**Fernando Perez-Villatoro** is a PhD student in bioinformatics at
University of Helsinki.

nformatician in multiplexed
immunofluorescence image analysis at University of Helsinki.

**Fernando Perez-Villatoro** is a PhD student in bioinformatics at
University of Helsinki. His research is focusing on the immune response
of the ovarian tumor microenvironment.

**Inga-Maria Launonen** is an MD PhD student at University of Helsinki.
Her research interest is delineating therapy responses in the tumor
microenvironment.

**Ella Anttila** is a BMed student at University of Helsinki.

**Kevin Elias** is a gynecologic oncologist and surgeon-scientist at
Dana-Farber and Brigham and Women\'s Hospital.

**Julia Casado** is a Postdoc at University of Helsinki. Her research
focused on the tumor microenvironment from highly multiplexed images of
tumor tissue.

**Peter K. Sorger** is a systems and cancer biologist and Otto Krayer
Professor of Systems Pharmacology in the Department of Systems Biology
at Harvard Medical School.

**Anniina Färkkilä** is an assistant professor in translational
gynecologic oncology and a Specialist in gynecology at the University of
Helsinki.

**Multiplexed imaging at single-cell resolution has been widely used to
decipher the role of the cellular microenvironment in cancer and other
complex diseases. Accurate cell-type phenotyping based on marker
combinations is crucial, but challenging because of (i) fluorescence

cellular microenvironment in cancer and other
complex diseases. Accurate cell-type phenotyping based on marker
combinations is crucial, but challenging because of (i) fluorescence
noise and batch effects, (ii) segmentation artifacts, (iii) laborious
annotation of the ground truth, and (iv) difficulty in reproducing
human-biased thresholding. Here, we present Tribus, an interactive
knowledge-based classifier designed for multiplexed image and proteomic
datasets that avoids hard-set thresholds and manual labeling. The
performance of Tribus was validated by benchmarking it with two similar
methods on four public datasets with manual labels. Tribus can recover
fine-grained cell types as accurately as human experts while allowing
the discovery of phenotypically new subtypes. Two large in-house
whole-slide image datasets were selected to show how Tribus performs in
practical applications. Tribus is an open-source package that enables
accurate phenotyping of single cells to facilitate biological discovery
from highly multiplexed images.**

Keywords: Cell-type phenotyping, Clustering, Cyclic Immunofluorescence
(CyCIF), Co-detection by indexing (CODEX)

# INTRODUCTION

Multiplexed imaging techniques at single-cell resolution levels, such as
cyclic immunofluorescence (CyCIF) [(Lin et al.,
2018)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?KROv5k), co-detection by

ed imaging techniques at single-cell resolution levels, such as
cyclic immunofluorescence (CyCIF) [(Lin et al.,
2018)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?KROv5k), co-detection by
indexing (CODEX) [(Black et al.,
2021)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?JGGE22), and multiplexed ion
beam imaging by time of flight (MIBI-TOF) [(Keren et al.,
2019)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?QIwFvh), have developed
significant advantages for understanding tissue architecture. Such
techniques allow researchers to measure dozens of proteins at
single-cell resolution while retaining the spatial information of origin
in the tissue section. These experimental workflows generate datasets
that include multiple features and provide novel insights into the
diversity of cellular phenotypes and tissue behaviors [(Spitzer & Nolan,
2016)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?8HFLes).

Cell-type annotation has become one of the most essential tasks and a
bottleneck for gaining full potential from multiplexed imaging
technologies. The main obstacles to cell-type phenotyping on multiplexed
images are the unexpected combinations of markers, signal noise, and
reproducibility limit caused by imperfect cell segmentation,
pre-processing artifacts, or tissue processing artifacts, such as
autofluorescence. It is difficult to apply popular scRNA-seq cell-type

mit caused by imperfect cell segmentation,
pre-processing artifacts, or tissue processing artifacts, such as
autofluorescence. It is difficult to apply popular scRNA-seq cell-type
callers [(Xie et al., 2021)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?QShDIL)
to multiplexed imaging datasets because they are not designed to
overcome segmentation noise, which does not exist in scRNA-seq data. The
order-of-magnitude difference in the number of features is also an
indispensable problem. Several markers can dominate the clustering
process in multiplexed imaging techniques, and thousands of genes have
been provided to scRNA-seq callers to overcome this problem.

Gating [(Staats et al.,
2019)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?7mgQgW)is a classical
cell-type annotation method that requires manual identification of cell
types based on the pattern of features visualized by histograms and
bivariate plots. However, with the increasing size of the marker panel,
manual gating becomes time-consuming and sensitive to batch effects
[(Verschoor et al., 2015)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?beDAnS),
which is no longer feasible. Unsupervised clustering tools such as
PhenoGraph [(Levine et al.,
2015)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?fqmsl5), Leiden [(Traag et
al., 2019)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?U71hm0), and DBscan

tools such as
PhenoGraph [(Levine et al.,
2015)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?fqmsl5), Leiden [(Traag et
al., 2019)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?U71hm0), and DBscan
[(Ester et al., 1996)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?L0oHCj) have
been applied to perform automatic data exploration [(Liu et al.,
2019)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?hnJI58). However, manual
checking is still required to assign meaningful cell types to the
obtained clusters. To perform a deeper profiling of cell types, it is
usually necessary to perform over-clustering and then merge the
clusters, which is both computationally and time-consuming. An
alternative approach is to use supervised or semi-supervised machine
learning methods [(Abdelaal et al.,
2019)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?49sLMo), which were developed
to perform accurate auto cell-type classification. However, such methods
require pre-training on manually gated datasets according to various
experimental designs, thus requiring extra human work and lacking
extendability.

Here, we introduce the design of a novel cell-type caller named the
Tribus. Tribus performs reproducible automatic cell-type annotations
across variable high-plex imaging and proteomic datasets without manual
thresholding. Tribus incorporates a widely used self-organizing map
(SOM) [(Kohonen, 1982)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?Yl4HID)

aging and proteomic datasets without manual
thresholding. Tribus incorporates a widely used self-organizing map
(SOM) [(Kohonen, 1982)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?Yl4HID)
unsupervised neural network model with a unique scoring function for
assigning cell types according to prior biological knowledge. An
integrated Napari plugin allows for fast user interaction and curation.
We validated the accuracy of Tribus on four public multiplexed imaging
and non-spatial proteomic datasets generated by CODEX and the mass
cytometry methods. We compared the performance of Tribus with two
similar knowledge-based cell-type identification approaches: ACDC [(Lee
et al., 2017)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?J4be42) and Astir
[(Geuenich et al., 2021)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?zlhfxw).
We demonstrate the utility of Tribus in the analysis of two in-house
CyCIF datasets. Overall, Tribus represents a new framework for
semi-automated cell-type calling for multiplexed imaging and proteomics
techniques.

# Methods

## The structure of Tribus

To run Tribus, a marker expression table and a prior knowledge-based
logic table are required as inputs. The logic table can be defined as a
data matrix $L(c_{i},\ m_{j})$ containing values
$\lbrack - 1,\ 0,\ 1\rbrack$, where $c_{i}$ is the cell type
${i_{th}}^{}$ and $m_{j}$ is the marker ${j_{th}}^{}$. If a marker is

data matrix $L(c_{i},\ m_{j})$ containing values
$\lbrack - 1,\ 0,\ 1\rbrack$, where $c_{i}$ is the cell type
${i_{th}}^{}$ and $m_{j}$ is the marker ${j_{th}}^{}$. If a marker is
present in a specific cell type, the value in the logic table is
assigned a value of 1. If a marker is supposed to be absent in a
specific cell type, then the value in the logic table should be assigned
as -1. For neutral or unknown markers, a score of 0 was assigned. Each
cell type should at least have one positive marker in the logic table
(Supplementary Table 1-3).

Tribus comprises two main parts. First, Tribus uses unsupervised
learning to find clusters from the input data, and then assigns each
cluster to a certain cell type based on the logic table. The cell type
assignment is performed hierarchically, which means Tribus first assigns
lower-level cell types and then assigns higher-level cell types to
create more precise categories.

## Unsupervised learning in Tribus

Tribus uses a self-organizing map (SOM) for clustering based on the
MiniSOM package [(Vettigli,
2013/2023)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?ghMcaA). SOM can
represent an input space with high dimensions as a map space, which
consists of components called \"nodes\". The quantization error (Q) was
used to evaluate the algorithm performance, which was calculated by

h high dimensions as a map space, which
consists of components called \"nodes\". The quantization error (Q) was
used to evaluate the algorithm performance, which was calculated by
determining the average distance of the sample vectors to the cluster
centroids.

[![](media/image2.png){width="1.5972222222222223in"
height="0.4305555555555556in"}](https://www.codecogs.com/eqnedit.php?latex=%20Q%20%3D%20%5Cfrac%7B1%7D%7Bm%7D%5Csum_%7Bq%3D1%7D%5E%7Bm%7D%7B%5C%7C%5Cboldsymbol%7B%5Cvec%7Bx%7D%5Eq%20-%20%5Cvec%7Bw_i%7D%5Eq%7D%5C%7C%7D%5E2%20#0)

A comparison of quantization errors needs to be performed under the same
grid size [(Pölzlbauer,
n.d.)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?7mS4PU). To determine the
optimal grid size, we used the approach of Vesanto [(Vesanto &
Alhoniemi, 2000)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?KEe5Lj): $G = 5$,
where $N$ is the input data size (i.e., number of cells). The parameters
of the SOM, including $\sigma$ (the spread of the neighborhood function)
and learning rate, were optimized by minimizing the quantization error.
Hyperparameter tuning was implemented based on the package hyperopt
[(Bergstra et al., 2013)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?fTIX2g),
where the objective function was to minimize quantization error, and the
search space for $\sigma$ and learning rate ranged from 0.001 to 5.

## Cell type assignment by scoring functions

e objective function was to minimize quantization error, and the
search space for $\sigma$ and learning rate ranged from 0.001 to 5.

## Cell type assignment by scoring functions

After SOM clustering, a node matrix $N(n_{k},\ v_{j})$ is generated,
where $v_{j}$ is calculated using the median expression of marker
$m_{j}$ in $n_{k}$. We designed a scoring function based on the concept
of squared error implemented in the QueryStarPlot function of the
FlowSOM package [(Van Gassen et al.,
2015)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?G09YkV). The function
calculates the score $s_{i}$ of a certain cell type $i$ for each node
$k$.

height="0.4444444444444444in"}](https://www.codecogs.com/eqnedit.php?latex=s_i(n_k)%20%3D%20%5Cfrac%7B1%7D%7Bk%7D%5Csum_%7Bj%7D%5B1-%7B(v_%7Bij%7D-%5Chat%7Bv_%7Bij%7D%7D)%5E2%7D'%5D#0)

where,

height="0.6604265091863517in"}

Instead of using the maximum value, the 99 percentile was chosen to make
the Tribus more robust to outliers. Assigning node $n_{k}$ to cell type
$c_{i}$ by: $c_{i} = argmax_{s_{i}}f(n_{k},\ s_{i})$

Note that if the maximum score of a cluster is smaller than a certain
threshold set by users, the cluster will be labeled as the \"other\"
cell type. Similarly, if the difference between the maximum and second

of a cluster is smaller than a certain
threshold set by users, the cluster will be labeled as the \"other\"
cell type. Similarly, if the difference between the maximum and second
maximum score is smaller than a certain threshold, the cluster will be
labeled as \"undefined\".

## Napari plug-in

To efficiently assess and explore cell-type labeling results, we
developed a custom plugin integrated with the Napari [(Ahlers, Jannis et
al., 2023)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?ZIFqeC) framework. This
allows the user to run Tribus over one sample at a time, display the
results simultaneously, or load previously saved data. We incorporated
the ZARR format to overcome the computational limitations associated
with large datasets.

The key functionalities of the Napari plugin include the following.

\(i\) Cell-type mask visualization: The plugin sorts and displays
different cell-type labels as separate layers using visually distinct
colors, allowing the user to overlay them with the imaging data for
quality control. This function is also available in a stand-alone
Jupyter Notebook.

\(ii\) Probability score visualization: Cell masks are represented as a
color gradient of the probability score assigned by the algorithm. This
allows the user to identify and check ambiguous cells. This allows the
user to identify and check ambiguous cells and assess the assigned

ity score assigned by the algorithm. This
allows the user to identify and check ambiguous cells. This allows the
user to identify and check ambiguous cells and assess the assigned
"other" and "undefined" thresholds.

\(iii\) Marker intensity visualization: The median expression levels of
the desired markers are represented through gradient shading on the
segmentation mask. This allows the user to visually assess the results
and look for potential biases.

## Benchmarking Datasets

We chose four public mass cytometry and multiplexed imaging datasets
with ground truth labels to test the performance of Tribus.

### AML

This dataset contains single-cell proteomic profiles of human bone
marrow from patients with acute myeloid leukemia (AML) and healthy adult
donors [(Levine et al.,
2015)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?a3cXqw). The AML dataset was
collected via mass cytometry using a panel of 32 markers targeting 14
manually gated cell classes. When performing benchmarking, the
\"NotDebrisSinglets\" cell type was removed from the analysis (N =
104184 cells). The cell typing logic table was designed based on the
cell type-marker table provided in ACDC research.

### BMMC

The BMMC dataset was obtained from bone marrow mononuclear cells
[(Bendall et al., 2011)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?NaqDnh) and
profiled using mass cytometry. The publicly available dataset contains

from bone marrow mononuclear cells
[(Bendall et al., 2011)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?NaqDnh) and
profiled using mass cytometry. The publicly available dataset contains
13 markers and 24 manually gated cell populations. Following ACDC
research, erythroblasts, megakaryocyte platelets, and myelocytes were
merged as an unknown population and removed from the analysis. All
\"NotGated\" cells were also removed (N = 61725 cells). The cell typing
logic table was modified based on the cell type-marker table provided in
ACDC research.

### DCIS

The ductal carcinoma in situ (DCIS) dataset was collected using
multiplexed ion beam imaging by time-of-flight (MIBI-TOF) with a 37-plex
antibody staining panel. DCIS consists of 69672 cells, 23 cell types
from tissues containing 79 clinically annotated surgical resections
[(Risom et al., 2022)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?dJSFis),
including normal breast tissue (N = 9, reduction mammoplasty), primary
DCIS (N = 58), and IBC (N = 12). The cell-typing logic table for DCIS
was also generated based on the panel design in the published research.

### HuBMAP

HuBMAP is a published co-detection by indexing (CODEX) imaging dataset
collected from nine donors with a 54-plex panel targeting 25 cell types
[(Hickey et al., 2023)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?SrSjJ5). The

y indexing (CODEX) imaging dataset
collected from nine donors with a 54-plex panel targeting 25 cell types
[(Hickey et al., 2023)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?SrSjJ5). The
HuBMAP dataset includes eight regions (in the order of trajectory from
the stomach): duodenum, proximal jejunum, mid-jejunum, and ileum from
the small bowel, and ascending, transverse, descending, and sigmoid
regions of the large intestine (colon). In this study, we used manually
labeled donor 004 from the HuBMAP dataset (total N = 110635 for four
colon regions, total N = 137652 for four small bowel regions). Cell
typing logic tables were generated according to the panel design
information provided in published studies.

### In-house Datasets

To further validate the performance of Tribus, we also applied Tribus to
two in-house high-grade serous ovarian cancer (HGSC) datasets generated
by tissue-based cyclic immunofluorescence (tCyCIF). The HRP dataset
contained 21 homologous recombination-proficient (HRP) HGSC samples
stained with a 14-plex antibody panel (Supplementary Table 4). The NACT
dataset contains three images, including two tumor sections after
neoadjuvant chemotherapy (NACT) and one treatment-naive biopsy. The NACT
dataset was stained using a 36-plex antibody panel (Supplementary Table
5).

Cell segmentation was carried out using StarDist, and the mean

nd one treatment-naive biopsy. The NACT
dataset was stained using a 36-plex antibody panel (Supplementary Table
5).

Cell segmentation was carried out using StarDist, and the mean
fluorescence intensities were quantified using in-house scripts. Hoechst
cycles were used to filter out the detached tissue areas and cells that
moved between cycles. Staining artifacts were removed by manual
cropping. The quantified data were further preprocessed: log
transformation, z-score normalization, 99.9 percentile outlier removal,
and co-factor 5 arcsinh transformation [(Hickey et al.,
2021)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?Tuc7St) were applied before
phenotype assignment. In the NACT dataset, 976,082 cells were annotated
using a cell phenotyping logic table based on panel design. For the HRP
dataset, approximately 10.5M cells were annotated using a constant logic
table.

## Methods for comparison and evaluation metrics

We compared the performance of Tribus with two similar prior
knowledge-based cell-type calling tools, ACDC [(Lee et al.,
2017)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?nMMQlc) and Astir [(Geuenich
et al., 2021)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?1m7okT). ACDC allows
prior knowledge-based cell type calling by generating landmark points
based on the knowledge table and performing semi-supervised
classification on single cells. Astir is a deep recognition neural

ased cell type calling by generating landmark points
based on the knowledge table and performing semi-supervised
classification on single cells. Astir is a deep recognition neural
network that performs fast inference of cell types based on prior
knowledge of the marker proteins.

We evaluated the overall clustering performance for all cell types by
comparing the Rand Index [(Rand,
1971)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?XrD40k), Accuracy, Precision
score (weighted), and F1 score (weighted) [(Powers,
2020)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?419Lek). All the above
metrics were calculated using the functions provided in Scikit-learn
[(Pedregosa et al., 2011)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?4aKZIs).

The Rand Index was chosen to measure the similarity between two data
clusters and was defined as $RI = (a + b)/(\frac{N}{2}$. Accuracy was
defined as $Accuracy\ = \ (TN + TP)/(TN + FN + TP + FP)$. F1-score can
reflect the harmonic mean of precision (purity) and recall (yield),

[![](media/image4.png){width="1.875in"
height="0.3611111111111111in"}](https://www.codecogs.com/eqnedit.php?latex=P_%7Bij%7D%20%3D%20%5Cfrac%7BC_%7Bij%7D%7D%7B%5Csum_k%7BC_%7Bik%7D%7D%7D%2C%20R_%7Bij%7D%20%3D%20%5Cfrac%7BC_%7Bij%7D%7D%7B%5Csum_k%7BC_%7Bkj%7D%7D%7D#0)

where $C_{ij}$ is the number of events classified as population $i$ that
belongs to the manually annotated population $j$.

# RESULTS

## Overview of Tribus

Tribus is a hierarchical framework for cell-type assignment in
multiplexed image datasets based on prior panel knowledge (Fig 1). The
prior knowledge is provided as a logic table, where markers are
specified as "positive", "negative" or "unknown" for each cell type.

When running the analysis, Tribus first performs unsupervised clustering
using a self-organizing map on the input dataset if the number of cells
in the given dataset is larger than the user-setting threshold.
Otherwise, Tribus directly calculates the scoring function for each cell
type at the single-cell level. The labeling process of cell-type
assignment using Tribus considers ambiguous results, which means that
Tribus does not force naming. If the scores of a cluster are lower than
a certain threshold for all cell types, it is marked as 'unknown'.

ders ambiguous results, which means that
Tribus does not force naming. If the scores of a cluster are lower than
a certain threshold for all cell types, it is marked as 'unknown'.
Similarly, if the cell type scores are closer than a threshold, the
cluster is marked as \"undefined\" to avoid incorrect assignments and
facilitate the identification of new cell types.

## Tribus recovers fine-grained cell types as accurately as human experts

To test whether Tribus can recover canonical cellular populations, we
applied Tribus to the benchmark dataset DCIS, which contained 23 cell
types from a cohort of 79 patients. Tribus successfully identified all
populations highlighted in the study (Supplementary Figure 1A), and the
median marker intensities of cellular populations were similar to those
of human-labeled populations (Fig 2A). One significant difference was
the population of MACS (macrophages) which showed a lower median
intensity of CD14 in Tribus than in manual gating. This might be because
CD14 was not constrained for MACS identification in DCIS research; CD14
was not constrained to be positive for MACS cells in the logic table.

To compare how the cell type varied between manual labels and Tribus
labels, we mapped cell masks back to the original tiff images with a
nine-color overlay of cell identity-related makers. The cell masks

ype varied between manual labels and Tribus
labels, we mapped cell masks back to the original tiff images with a
nine-color overlay of cell identity-related makers. The cell masks
looked similar when we checked all major cell types (Fig 2B). We then
validated the MACS population by visualizing the original images using
six related markers. We observed that more cells were assigned to MACS
in manual labels; however, the corresponding MACS markers were absent in
some cells (Fig 2B). With Tribus, we identified fewer MACS cases, but
with canonical marker combinations.

Comparing manual labels and Tribus labels, supervised UMAP visualization
of all cell types from DCIS datasets showed that Tribus could accurately
annotate the majority of cells (Fig 2C). Using the manual labels as
ground truth, Tribus achieved precision scores between 0.7 and 0.8,
average recalls around 0.6, and F1 scores between 0.6 and 0.7 across
most of the cell types (Supplementary Figure 1B).

## Tribus can target ambiguous populations and discover phenotypically new subtypes

One challenge for prior knowledge-based cell-type calling methods is to
discover ambiguous categories that are difficult to predefine in the
logic table. For example, there are groups of unknown cells with low
intensity among all the markers. The DCIS dataset is a good example of

that are difficult to predefine in the
logic table. For example, there are groups of unknown cells with low
intensity among all the markers. The DCIS dataset is a good example of
exploring ambiguous populations because it includes "IMMUNEOTHER" and
"OTHER" cell types in manual gating. Among them, the \"OTHER\" cell type
shows low marker intensity in all markers while \"IMMUNEOTHER\" was only
highly expressed in CD44 and CD45.

We verified that Tribus can successfully target ambiguous populations by
setting the decision thresholds in the scoring function. We explored
different settings of thresholds and compared their effects on Tribus
performance (Supplementary Figure 2A-B). Based on the result, we chose
the undefined_threshold of 0.001 and other_threshold of 0.04 in the
analysis of DCIS. From the UMAP visualization, we could observe the same
"IMMUNEOTHER" and "OTHER" cell clusters with the same local structures
being kept (Fig 2C). The marker expression heatmap shows that
Tribus-targeted ambiguous cell types have the same marker expression
profile as manually gated cell types, such as "IMMUNEOTHER" and "OTHER"
(Fig 2D). We also discovered new clusters from the UMAP plots
highlighted with orange boxes (Fig 2C), including cell types of
undefined tumors, fibroblasts, and T-cells.

Novel marker expression combinations were observed using a median marker

ghlighted with orange boxes (Fig 2C), including cell types of
undefined tumors, fibroblasts, and T-cells.

Novel marker expression combinations were observed using a median marker
expression heatmap (Fig 2D). We validated our observation by mapping
cell masks back to tiff images (Supplementary Figure 3 A-C). We
identified CD36+CD31+ fibroblasts and HER2- tumor luminal subtypes.
Undefined T-cells have phenotypically higher marker expression than
typical CD4T cells. We suggest that Tribus be considered as a starting
point to uncover novel cell states via further unsupervised clustering.

## Tribus outperformed other similar methods

We benchmarked the performance of Tribus using two other approaches,
ACDC and Astir.

For AML and BMMC datasets, we used the knowledge tables provided by ACDC
research and reformatted the logic tables/yaml files accordingly for
Tribus/Astir. No knowledge tables are available for the DCIS and HubMAP
datasets; thus we generated the knowledge tables/files for all methods
based on the panel information. The ACDC analysis was performed with
parameters (n_neighbor = 10, thres = 0.5) in the example scripts. The
parameters for Astir analysis were chosen (max_epochs = 1000,
learning_rate = 2e-3, initial_epochs = 3) based on the tutorial provided
in Colab.

Benchmarking experiments showed that Tribus outperformed the other

e chosen (max_epochs = 1000,
learning_rate = 2e-3, initial_epochs = 3) based on the tutorial provided
in Colab.

Benchmarking experiments showed that Tribus outperformed the other
methods in terms of efficiency and accuracy (Fig 3A). In the AML and
BMMC datasets, Tribus reached a similar accuracy to ACDC, but with a
shorter running time of one order of magnitude (Fig 3B). Tribus
outperforms other methods across metrics on highly multiplexed imaging
datasets (Fig 3C), such as DCIS and HubMAP, and has comparable
performance in non-multiplexed AML and BMMC datasets. Tribus
successfully identified all the cell types highlighted in the four
public datasets (Supplementary Figure 4 A-D).

We then explored how the structure of the logic table affected the
accuracy. Using the AML dataset as an example, we applied Tribus
analysis with (1) a logic table with only one global level and (2) a
logic table that includes major cell types at the global level and
sub-phenotypes (for example, CD16- and CD16+ NK cells) at the second
level (Supplementary Table 1-3). We repeated the Tribus analysis five
times, calculated the average metrics, and visualized the results for
each logic table. When adjusting the logic table for the AML dataset,
using a hierarchical logic table can help improve the accuracy and
increase the F1 score by 0.1 (Fig 3D).

or
each logic table. When adjusting the logic table for the AML dataset,
using a hierarchical logic table can help improve the accuracy and
increase the F1 score by 0.1 (Fig 3D).

## Tribus yields rapid and accurate cell type calling in large whole slide imaging datasets

For the HRP dataset, we used a constant one-level logic table for all 21
samples, intending to identify tumor, stromal, CD4 Tcell, CD8 Tcell,
CD11c+ myeloids, and IBA1+myeloids (Supplementary Figure 5A). We applied
Tribus to the NACT dataset guided by a four-level logic table
(Supplementary Figure 5B).

In the HRP dataset, Tribus accurately identified six cell types and the
cellular proportion showed high heterogeneity among patients (Fig 4A).
In the NACT dataset, Tribus successfully identified major cell
phenotypes as well as subtypes that we were able to characterize based
on the available marker panel (Fig 4B). The UMAP projection shows clear
cell-type populations in the HRP and NACT datasets (Fig 4C-D). We also
visualized the UMAP projection colored by sample origin on the HRP and
NACT datasets (Supplementary Figure 5C-D), and the results indicated a
low batch effect in the analysis.

We showed that Tribus can successfully annotate dense tissue areas and
create accurate phenotype labels for further analysis. The multiplexed
tCyCIF image from sample 06 of the NACT dataset showed the nuclei and

sfully annotate dense tissue areas and
create accurate phenotype labels for further analysis. The multiplexed
tCyCIF image from sample 06 of the NACT dataset showed the nuclei and
representative tumor and immune markers (Fig 4E). Tribus could
accurately identify the CD20+, CD8a+, and CD4+ cell populations within a
Tertiary Lymphoid Structure (TLS) despite the dense organization of
these sub-phenotypes (Fig 4F). Tribus also annotated the proliferating
subpopulation of tumor cells and tumor-infiltrating CD8+ cells from a
dense area (Fig 4G).

# DISCUSSION

Cell-type calling and feature extraction are critical steps in
high-dimensional image analysis. The growing complexity and increasing
number of panels in high-dimensional data necessitate the development of
reproducible and more automated cell-type calling approaches. In this
study, we developed Tribus, which is a semi-supervised cell-type calling
analysis framework for multiplexed imaging datasets. Tribus has
advantages in terms of efficiency, accuracy, user-friendliness, and
reproducibility.

Tribus was generated as an automatic cell-type caller for
high-dimensional data by incorporating biological knowledge from the
panel design into the analysis. This was done through carefully designed
scoring functions based on marker expression per grid generated by
unsupervised machine learning, to minimize bias. When the number of

is. This was done through carefully designed
scoring functions based on marker expression per grid generated by
unsupervised machine learning, to minimize bias. When the number of
cells included in the clusters was lower than the threshold set by the
user, Tribus skipped generating the clusters and calculated the scores
at the single-cell level. This flexible scoring function calculation
strategy enables the discovery of rare cell types. Tribus was integrated
with Napari, and a plugin was provided to import the cell-type
identification result in one click, which significantly increased the
simplicity of interactive quality control. Such integration enables
convenient operation for users who are not familiar with programming and
the interpretation of biological contexts. Tribus was specifically
crafted for high-plex imaging and proteomic technologies and was
seamlessly operated across various imaging platforms.

However, Tribus is not without limitations. Tribus performance is highly
related to the quality of the input dataset and prior knowledge of the
expected cell types in the user-defined initial cell-type logic table.
To assign a uniform logic table, the samples should have even staining
patterns, both within and across slides. Uneven staining patterns and
antibodies with suboptimal signal-to-noise ratios can heavily affect the
results of Tribus.

have even staining
patterns, both within and across slides. Uneven staining patterns and
antibodies with suboptimal signal-to-noise ratios can heavily affect the
results of Tribus. In sub-optimal cases, the user can create a
hierarchical logic table in such a way that major cell phenotypes with
clear marker signals are separated first on different levels. This can
result in more accurate labels if the distinct areas of the image are
affected. Tribus cannot find cell types that do not exist in the input
logic table, but it can return undefined cell types for further
exploration with unsupervised clustering methods.

Moreover, cell-type annotation from bioimages has inherent challenges
owing to imperfect cell segmentation and collateral spillover. Expanding
nuclei masks by a few pixels can enhance cytoplasmic marker visibility,
as it allows better capture of the signal when cells express these
markers. However, in dense tissue areas, this might increase spillover
which highlights the importance of the hierarchically structured logic
table. Careful design of the table can aid cell separation as only a
subset of cells are taken into account on the lower levels during the
phenotype assignment. Typically, mild spillover affects only part of a
cell, whereas a true signal yields a more uniform expression pattern and
higher mean fluorescence intensity.

phenotype assignment. Typically, mild spillover affects only part of a
cell, whereas a true signal yields a more uniform expression pattern and
higher mean fluorescence intensity. Tribus was designed to include both
positive and negative components of the expected marker expression. We
could also aid correct labeling in the case of a mild spillover using a
less strict logic table, where markers with false positive expressions
are set as neutral.

Overall, we propose Tribus as a fast, accurate, and user-friendly
cell-type identification method that can be integrated into multiplexed
image-analysis frameworks.

# Key points

- A semi-supervised cell-type identification computational tool for
 > imaging data was developed, enabling fast and accurate phenotyping
 > of single cells.

- The tool was integrated with the Napari plug-in to accomplish a
 > user-friendly assessment of the resulting quality before
 > downstream analysis.

- Compared with other similar methods, our method performed the best
 > on all benchmark datasets.

- Tribus avoids hard thresholding and allows the capture of ambiguous
 > cell populations, thus enabling novel biological discovery in
 > downstream analysis.
